summary: Ronacher describes his work-from-home setup and why he keeps it intentionally plain. He favors standardized, mass-produced gear for easy replacement and minimal friction, even if it is less “custom.” Docking his MacBook remains surprisingly finicky, and he opts for a stable, practical hub chain over idealized setups. He has simplified his workflow over time, dropping niche ergonomics and multi-monitor complexity to stay portable and consistent. The conclusion is that boring, standardized choices plus small targeted improvements yield the most reliable daily experience.
key_points: Standardized, widely available hardware reduces downtime and travel friction.
USB-C docking is still unreliable; stability sometimes requires inelegant adapter chains.
He deliberately moved away from complex customizations and multi-monitor setups.
He invests in small quality-of-life improvements like cable management and desk automation.
money_quotes: Mostly because after seeing so many complex dev setups recently I wanted to share something that's largely bland.
I ended up with a pretty straightforward Anker USB-C hub I use for power, and into it I plugged some Chinese off-brand multiport.
It turns out in my case chaining multiple adapters is more stable than the alternatives.
Over the last few years my development setup has become more and more boring.
So I optimized towards getting the most out of the most widely available devices.
On the other hand I'm obsessed with small improvements.
Considering how long it takes to move up and down this got old, so I wrote a script to automate this.
key_insight: A deliberately boring, standardized setup—paired with small automation tweaks—beats complex customizations for reliability and focus.
